task: I’ve got to send a short note to a subcontractor about a missed step on a job, and I want it to be clear enough that there’s no confusion about what needs to happen next. Can you help me word it so the message stays professional and gets the point across quickly?

prefs.short: Wants blunt answers without hedging or fluff
prefs.explanation: He comes across as someone who values straight talk and dislikes being softened up. Given his hard-nosed, efficiency-first style, he is likely to prefer clear guidance over polite ambiguity. He probably sees excessive caution in wording as a waste of time.

prefs.short: Prefers concise responses that get to the point
prefs.explanation: His work style is all about no wasted motion, so he is likely to want answers that are brief and useful. Long explanations would probably feel like drag unless he specifically asks for them. He would rather get the key action than read a lot of setup.

prefs.short: Likes information laid out in simple bullets or checklists
prefs.explanation: Someone who manages jobsite logistics and paperwork would likely respond well to organized, scannable formats. Bullets and checklists fit his practical mindset because they make next steps obvious. He would probably dislike meandering prose when he needs to act quickly.

His persona suggests he does not want warmth or cheerfulness layered on top of the message. A steady, factual tone matches how he handles problems and authority. He would likely find overly friendly or emotionally expressive help distracting.

prefs.short: Can take firm pushback if it protects the job or avoids mistakes
prefs.explanation: He is used to being corrected by procedures, inspections, and hard realities, so he likely accepts pushback when it is justified. What matters to him is whether the objection is practical and protects outcomes, not whether it feels pleasant. He would probably reject soft disagreement that sounds indecisive or preachy.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a short, professional version you can send:

**Hi [Subcontractor Name],**  
We noticed that [missed step] was not completed on [job/project]. Please take care of this as soon as possible and confirm once it’s done. Let me know if you have any questions.

If you want, I can also give you:
- a **firmer version**
- a **more diplomatic version**
- or a version tailored to the specific missed step.

per_pref.reason: Mostly blunt and direct: "We noticed that [missed step] was not completed" and "Please take care of this as soon as possible". Slight softening from "Absolutely" and "If you want" keeps it from being maximally blunt.
Very concise and to the point. It gives the note in a few sentences and avoids extra explanation.
Uses a simple, scannable structure with a short draft and bullet options: "a firmer version / a more diplomatic version / a version tailored...". Not a full checklist, but clearly organized.
Tone is matter-of-fact and professional, e.g. "We noticed... was not completed" and "Please take care of this". The opening "Absolutely" is a bit friendly, but overall it stays no-nonsense.

Includes firm, practical pushback without being preachy: it states the missed step and asks for completion and confirmation. The offer of a "firmer version" also shows willingness to escalate firmness if needed.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a firmer, ready-to-send version:

**Hi [Subcontractor Name],**  
You missed **[step]** on **[job]**. Complete it immediately and confirm once it’s done.

If you want, I can make it even tighter or turn it into a text-message version.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt: "You missed [step] on [job]. Complete it immediately and confirm once it’s done." No hedging or softening.
Concise and to the point; the actual note is only two sentences plus an optional offer.
Uses a simple ready-to-send blockquote format, though not bullets/checklists specifically.
Matter-of-fact and no-nonsense: "firmer, ready-to-send version" and direct instruction language, with no warmth or cheerfulness.
Includes firm pushback/correction—"You missed [step]" and "Complete it immediately"—which clearly corrects the subcontractor without being indecisive.

turns.agent_response:
That works. Here’s a clean version you can send:

**[Name], you missed [step] on [job]. Get it done now and confirm when complete.**

If you want, I can also make it:
- even more blunt
- include a deadline
- or add a consequence without sounding sloppy.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt: "you missed [step]" and "Get it done now" with no hedging or softening.
Concise and to the point; gives a ready-to-send line in one sentence.
Uses a simple quoted template plus a short bullet list of options, which is easy to scan.
Matter-of-fact and no-nonsense: "clean version" and direct instruction language, no warmth or cheerfulness.
Includes firm pushback/pressure with "Get it done now" and offers to add "a consequence," showing willingness to be firm when needed.
